title: Computational Humor Modeling: A Survey on the State of the Art
research_questions: What are the current trends, advancements, and remaining gaps in recent computational humor modeling research with AI systems?

How do recent datasets, shared tasks, detection models, generation models, annotation practices, evaluation methods, and explainability work compare across humor tasks, modalities, genres, and languages?
To what extent do gaps identified by earlier computational humor surveys—evaluation, links to humor theory, English/text-only focus, and multimodality—remain relevant?

datasets_used.name: Political parody in social media
datasets_used.size: 131,666 English tweets from 184 real politicians and their parody accounts

datasets_used.name: CHEESE!
datasets_used.size: audio and video recordings of 11 human-human interactions in French that each last approximately 15 minutes

datasets_used.name: OpenMic
datasets_used.size: over 40 hours of annotated open mic clips

datasets_used.name: Tanaka et al. meme humor dataset
Hindi meme humor dataset (Kumari et al. 2024)
Multimodal and multilingual stand-up comedy corpus
datasets_used.size: 37 hours of video material scraped from YouTube

datasets_used.name: HAHA@IberLEF 2021 dataset
datasets_used.size: 36,000 tweets (24,000 for training, and 6,000 for development and testing, each), of which 15,000 were humorous

datasets_used.name: CLEF 2024 JOKER Task 1 dataset
datasets_used.size: more than 60,000 English texts, of which 4,492 were humorous

datasets_used.name: JOKER-2023 wordplay corpus
datasets_used.size: 1,405 English wordplay instances, with 5,838 translations to French

methods_proposed.name: Survey collection and filtering methodology
methods_proposed.summary: The paper collects recent computational humor work using keyword search across Google Scholar, Semantic Scholar, ACL Anthology, ACM Computing Surveys, IEEE Xplore, and the European Journal of Humor Research, supplemented by backward and forward citation search. Inclusion is governed by a decision tree requiring relevance to computational humor modeling, recent publication, peer review or specified exceptions, and special rules for shared tasks.

methods_proposed.name: Multidimensional evaluation template for humor generation
methods_proposed.summary: As a future evaluation direction, the authors propose evaluating generative humor models along diversity in humor mechanisms, appropriateness in pragmatic and social context, and originality. They argue that the validity of such a paradigm should be tested by correlation with human evaluations.

humor_theories.detail: The survey uses Martin and Ford’s distinction between jokes, performance humor, and conversational humor to classify recent humor datasets.
The survey repeatedly discusses incongruity as a basis for feature engineering, meme-caption modeling, pun recognition, surprise measures, and theory-driven detection/generation systems.
The paper states that Liu and Hou applied incongruity theory of humor, specifically Script-Based Semantic Theory, to verbal humor using setup uncertainty and setup-punchline incongruity.
The survey discusses surprise-disambiguation theory in THInC, Tian et al.’s pun generation framework, and PunIntended, as well as distribution-based surprise measures for humorous headlines.
Superiority theory is included among the humor theories represented in the THInC ensemble discussed by the survey.
Relief theory is included among the humor theories represented in THInC, and the survey reports that THInC’s ablation study found relief theory contributed most to the model’s performance.
The survey states that Li et al.’s attention analyses aligned with benign violation theory, but describes the alignment as correlational rather than symbolic or rule-based.

evaluation_methods.detail: The survey reports that humor detection is typically evaluated with precision, recall, F1-score, and/or accuracy, with less frequent use of AUC, TPR, TNR, cross-entropy loss, Cohen’s Kappa, Pearson/Spearman correlation, RMSE, BLEU, ROUGE, BERTScore, Dist-n, and NDCG depending on task.

For generation, the survey discusses human comparison, A/B testing, binary success rate, qualitative analysis, and scoring of fine-grained aspects such as funniness, creativity/originality/authenticity, ambiguity, offensiveness, persuasiveness, coherence, relevance/informativeness, overall score, and Creativity Support Index.

The survey discusses work using GPT-4 to evaluate jokes and to judge pun explanations, while noting weak correlation with human evaluators in one study and risks of hallucinated or superficial explanations.
The survey describes datasets using crowd or user signals, including Facebook Haha reactions in CHoRaL and Amazon Mechanical Turk judgments for comparing human- and ChatGPT-generated humor.

The paper discusses annotation practices involving multiple annotators, demographic reporting, intended versus perceived humor labels, and offensiveness annotations to account for subjectivity and personal sensitivities.

headline_results.finding: The survey found that most recent humor datasets contain jokes.
The survey found a substantial recent shift toward multimodal humor datasets.
Conversational humor is underrepresented in datasets despite being the largest share of daily humor experiences cited by the paper.
The SemEval-2020 Task 7 winner used an ensemble of pre-trained transformers and won both subtasks.
headline_results.best_system: ensemble model consisting of various pre-trained transformers

headline_results.finding: In HaHackathon, top systems performed much better on intended humor detection than on humor and offensiveness rating.
On New Yorker caption tasks, few-shot GPT-4 was best among reported models but below human performance.
headline_results.value: 85% and 73%, compared to a human upper bound of 94% and human performance of 87%

key_findings: Recent computational humor datasets remain dominated by jokes, while conversational humor is proportionally underrepresented relative to its role in daily humor experiences.
Multimodal humor work has grown, especially for detection, with fusion models commonly combining BERT-based text representations with visual and audio encoders such as ViT/CLIP and HuBERT/Wav2Vec.
Humor generation papers since 2022 all use pre-trained language models, but findings are mixed because results are prompt-dependent and evaluation practices are not standardized.

Detection research shows no single dominant architecture: pre-trained language models, older neural methods, statistical models, and hybrid feature-based systems are all still used, sometimes competitively on specific tasks.
Recent datasets show a positive trend away from English-only research, with the survey stating that all 11 identified humor datasets published in 2024 contain at least 1 non-English language.
Interpretability and explainability work suggests that high-performing humor systems often rely on superficial cues, and LLM free-text explanations can be plausible but unfaithful or lacking nuance.
limitations: The survey focuses on manuscripts published since 2022, while dataset and shared task papers are included only from January 2020 onward.
Papers tailored specifically towards irony and sarcasm are excluded.
Non-peer-reviewed papers are included only if the presented model is patented or if code/data was shared and novel experiments were presented.
For shared task papers, the survey includes only overview papers or papers of winning teams.
Because the field of AI is evolving rapidly, the survey’s state-of-the-art snapshot is time-bounded by the search and inclusion criteria.
safety_ethics_notes: The survey repeatedly highlights humor subjectivity, personal and cultural sensitivities, offensive and hurtful humor, prejudice-spreading humor, and hateful meme risks. It presents HaHackathon as a positive step because it combines humor detection with offensiveness detection and discusses MemeCraft’s automatic filtering of offensive memes and post-production hatefulness detection as safety mechanisms.

judges.name: Jens Lemmens and Victor De Marez
judges.detail: The survey authors collected and filtered related work using keyword searches, backward/forward citation search, and the decision tree in Figure 1; they also categorized datasets/models by task, modality, genre, language, evaluation method, and humor-theory usage in the survey tables.

generation_selection.scope: Original study in this survey paper
generation_selection.detail: The paper is a literature survey and does not perform original humor generation experiments or select generated outputs.

inference_budgets.scope: Original study in this survey paper
inference_budgets.detail: No original model inference is reported; models named in the paper are models from reviewed studies rather than systems run by the survey authors.

human_baselines.scope: Original study in this survey paper
human_baselines.detail: The survey reports human baselines/evaluations from reviewed papers in some cases, but does not conduct an original human-vs-model evaluation under matched conditions.

contamination_risk.basis: This is a survey with no original model benchmarking or inference. Although it summarizes public datasets, shared tasks, and LLM studies from the literature, contamination risk for a newly run model evaluation is not applicable to the survey’s own evidence.

llm_judge_dependence.detail: The paper discusses reviewed work that used GPT-4 or other LLMs for evaluation/explanation, but the survey’s own conclusions are based on literature review and author synthesis, not on LLM judging.

reporting_gaps: The paper reports search sources and keywords but not exact search strings, query combinations, or search dates/cutoff beyond publication-scope statements.
The paper does not report the number of records retrieved, screened, excluded, or included at each stage of the survey selection process.

The paper does not report whether paper screening, inclusion decisions, or dataset/model categorizations were performed independently by multiple reviewers, nor any inter-rater agreement for those decisions.
The paper does not provide a PRISMA-style flow diagram or equivalent audit trail for exclusions.
No original model runs are performed, so exact model snapshots, inference dates, sampling parameters, token budgets, compute, or cost are not applicable rather than reported.
